vocabulary.en: to increase or intensify
vocabulary.excerpt: The U.S. military has ramped up attacks targeting Iranian roads, tunnels, railroads...
vocabulary.example_cn: 美军加大了对伊朗道路、隧道、铁路的打击力度。

vocabulary.en: the action of returning a military attack; revenge
vocabulary.excerpt: Iran expanded its list of targets across seven countries, including a U.S. base in Jordan, where two service members were killed Friday. Another is missing.
vocabulary.example_cn: 伊朗扩大了其目标清单，涵盖七个国家，包括约旦的一个美军基地。

vocabulary.en: to explode or cause to explode
vocabulary.excerpt: One U.S. service member also died in Northern Iraq Sunday while trying to detonate a downed Iranian attack drone.
vocabulary.example_cn: 一名美军士兵周日在伊拉克北部试图引爆一架被击落的伊朗攻击无人机时身亡。

vocabulary.en: in a way that is a permanent and essential part of something
vocabulary.excerpt: Nothing the military does is safe. It's all inherently dangerous.
vocabulary.example_cn: 军队所做的任何事都不安全，本质上都是危险的。

vocabulary.en: to suffer the worst part of something
vocabulary.excerpt: Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of the Iranian retaliations to these U.S. strikes.
vocabulary.example_cn: 科威特和巴林正承受着伊朗对美国打击报复的主要压力。

vocabulary.en: a situation in which you do something bad to someone because they did something bad to you
vocabulary.excerpt: It's very much a tit-for-tat. So, if the U.S. hits, you know, any sort of civilian infrastructure, the Iranians are going to do the same.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这很大程度上是以牙还牙。所以，如果美国打击任何民用基础设施，伊朗也会这样做。

vocabulary.en: a written record of an agreement that has not yet been formally signed
vocabulary.excerpt: The memorandum signed between the presidents of Iran and the U.S. once again proved to everyone how worthless and unreliable the signature of the U.S. president is.
vocabulary.example_cn: 伊朗和美国总统签署的备忘录再次向所有人证明了美国总统的签名是多么毫无价值和不可靠。

vocabulary.en: badly damaged or ruined
vocabulary.excerpt: But with the MOU now in tatters, there's no way to kind of enforce that piece of the agreement.
vocabulary.example_cn: 但随着谅解备忘录现在支离破碎，无法执行那部分协议。

vocabulary.en: strong and convincing; making you feel certain that something is true or that you must do something about it
vocabulary.excerpt: The president and this administration has really struggled to make a sustained, compelling case for this war.
vocabulary.example_cn: 总统和本届政府确实很难为这场战争提出持续且令人信服的理由。

vocabulary.en: to destroy something completely so that nothing is left
vocabulary.excerpt: Because the U.S. has obliterated Iran's military.
vocabulary.example_cn: 因为美国已经摧毁了伊朗的军队。

vocabulary.en: a combination of statements, ideas, or features that are opposed to one another
vocabulary.excerpt: And this goes back to the same contradiction around the nuclear program.
vocabulary.example_cn: 这又回到了围绕核计划的同一个矛盾。

vocabulary.en: to increase the proportion of a particular isotope in (a material), especially to make nuclear fuel
vocabulary.excerpt: making sure that Iran cannot enrich its uranium to develop a nuclear program.
vocabulary.example_cn: 确保伊朗无法浓缩铀以发展核计划。

vocabulary.en: to bring a feeling, a memory or an image into your mind
vocabulary.excerpt: again to try and evoke the idea of something being obliterated.
vocabulary.example_cn: 再次试图唤起某种被摧毁的想法。

vocabulary.en: to embrace or engage with something enthusiastically or aggressively
vocabulary.excerpt: Democrats are starting to really lean into the terminology 'war of choice.'
vocabulary.example_cn: 民主党人开始真正倾向于使用“选择性战争”这一术语。

vocabulary.en: a war that is entered into voluntarily, rather than in response to an attack or a direct threat
vocabulary.excerpt: Democrats are starting to really lean into the terminology 'war of choice.'
vocabulary.example_cn: 民主党人开始真正倾向于使用“选择性战争”这一术语。

vocabulary.en: the act of sending a person accused of a crime to the country where the crime was committed to be tried
vocabulary.excerpt: They're facing an extradition request from the U.K. on charges including rape and sex trafficking.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们面临英国提出的引渡请求，指控包括强奸和性贩卖。

vocabulary.en: the illegal trade of people for the purpose of sexual exploitation
vocabulary.excerpt: They're facing an extradition request from the U.K. on charges including rape and sex trafficking.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们面临英国提出的引渡请求，指控包括强奸和性贩卖。

vocabulary.en: a collection of websites, blogs, and online forums promoting masculinity and often misogyny
vocabulary.excerpt: because of their connections to like, the political manosphere?
vocabulary.example_cn: 因为他们与政治男性圈的联系？

vocabulary.en: to pretend not to notice something bad or illegal
vocabulary.excerpt: I don't know if they're going to turn a blind eye or not.
vocabulary.example_cn: 我不知道他们是否会视而不见。

vocabulary.en: a trick or device intended to attract attention, publicity, or trade
vocabulary.excerpt: But I think that there's no way, to Julia's point, that this is something that is -- we should be thinking about as more than a gimmick.
vocabulary.example_cn: 但我认为，正如朱莉娅所说，这绝不仅仅是一个噱头。

vocabulary.en: a group of people in a particular area who are represented by an elected official; also, a group of supporters
vocabulary.excerpt: New York has very different constituencies.
vocabulary.example_cn: 纽约有非常不同的选民群体。

vocabulary.en: to cause people to divide into two groups with completely opposite opinions
vocabulary.excerpt: Can you run a long-term mayoral tenure while polarizing literally half the city?
vocabulary.example_cn: 你能在让半个城市两极分化的情况下长期担任市长吗？

vocabulary.en: to show off or demonstrate one's power, influence, or ability
vocabulary.excerpt: And he's very interested in flexing that influence.
vocabulary.example_cn: 他非常热衷于展示那种影响力。

vocabulary.en: to use or take advantage of something
vocabulary.excerpt: So, he's continuing, even if it's not legal, to tap into that.
vocabulary.example_cn: 所以，即使不合法，他也在继续利用这一点。

vocabulary.en: a serious lung infection caused by the Legionella bacteria
vocabulary.excerpt: Plus, a deadly Legionnaires outbreak is growing in New York.
vocabulary.example_cn: 此外，纽约的致命军团病疫情正在扩大。

vocabulary.en: the sudden start of something unpleasant, especially a disease or violence
vocabulary.excerpt: Plus, a deadly Legionnaires outbreak is growing in New York.
vocabulary.example_cn: 此外，纽约的致命军团病疫情正在扩大。

vocabulary.en: to destroy a large part of something
vocabulary.excerpt: how is Iran able to strike back if it's decimated.
vocabulary.example_cn: 如果伊朗被摧毁，它如何能够反击？

vocabulary.en: continuing for a long time or without interruption
vocabulary.excerpt: The president and this administration has really struggled to make a sustained, compelling case for this war.
vocabulary.example_cn: 总统和本届政府确实很难为这场战争提出持续且令人信服的理由。

vocabulary.en: feeling tired or bored because you have lost interest in something
vocabulary.excerpt: the public is becoming more and more weary of conflicts in general.
vocabulary.example_cn: 公众总体上对冲突越来越厌倦。

vocabulary.en: to find faults or weaknesses in an argument or plan
vocabulary.excerpt: Because there's a lot of people poking holes.
vocabulary.example_cn: 因为有很多人在挑毛病。

sentences.en: The U.S. military has ramped up attacks targeting Iranian roads, tunnels, railroads, and, according to Iranian media, a plant for drinking water.
sentences.cn: 美军加大了对伊朗道路、隧道、铁路以及据伊朗媒体报道的一家饮用水厂的打击力度。
sentences.structure: 主语 (The U.S. military) + 谓语 (has ramped up) + 宾语 (attacks) + 现在分词短语作定语 (targeting...) + 插入语 (according to Iranian media)
sentences.analysis: 现在分词短语 'targeting...' 修饰 'attacks'，说明打击的目标。'according to Iranian media' 是插入语，表示信息来源。

sentences.en: One U.S. service member also died in Northern Iraq Sunday while trying to detonate a downed Iranian attack drone.
sentences.cn: 一名美军士兵周日在伊拉克北部试图引爆一架被击落的伊朗攻击无人机时身亡。
sentences.structure: 主语 (One U.S. service member) + 谓语 (died) + 地点状语 (in Northern Iraq) + 时间状语 (Sunday) + 时间状语从句省略 (while trying to detonate...)
sentences.excerpt: while trying to detonate... 是 while he was trying to detonate... 的省略形式，表示动作同时发生。

sentences.en: Nothing the military does is safe. It's all inherently dangerous.
sentences.cn: 军队所做的任何事都不安全，本质上都是危险的。
sentences.structure: 主语 (Nothing) + 定语从句 (the military does) + 系动词 (is) + 表语 (safe). 第二句：主语 (It) + 系动词 (is) + 副词 (all inherently) + 表语 (dangerous).
sentences.analysis: 第一句 'Nothing the military does' 是主语，'the military does' 是定语从句修饰 'Nothing'。第二句 'all inherently' 修饰 'dangerous'。

sentences.en: Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of the Iranian retaliations to these U.S. strikes.
sentences.cn: 科威特和巴林正承受着伊朗对美国打击报复的主要压力。
sentences.structure: 主语 (Kuwait and Bahrain) + 谓语 (are bearing) + 宾语 (the brunt) + 介词短语 (of the Iranian retaliations to these U.S. strikes)
sentences.analysis: 'bear the brunt of' 是固定短语，意为“承受主要压力”。'to these U.S. strikes' 说明报复的对象。

sentences.en: The memorandum signed between the presidents of Iran and the U.S. once again proved to everyone how worthless and unreliable the signature of the U.S. president is.
sentences.cn: 伊朗和美国总统签署的备忘录再次向所有人证明了美国总统的签名是多么毫无价值和不可靠。
sentences.structure: 主语 (The memorandum) + 过去分词短语作定语 (signed between...) + 谓语 (proved) + 间接宾语 (to everyone) + 宾语从句 (how worthless and unreliable the signature of the U.S. president is)
sentences.analysis: 过去分词短语 'signed between...' 修饰 'memorandum'。'how worthless and unreliable...' 是宾语从句，作 'proved' 的直接宾语。

sentences.en: But with the MOU now in tatters, there's no way to kind of enforce that piece of the agreement.
sentences.cn: 但随着谅解备忘录现在支离破碎，无法执行那部分协议。
sentences.structure: 介词短语 (with the MOU now in tatters) + there be 句型 (there's no way) + 不定式短语 (to kind of enforce that piece of the agreement)
sentences.analysis: 'with the MOU now in tatters' 是独立主格结构，表示伴随情况。'kind of' 是口语化表达，意为“有点，某种程度上”。

sentences.en: The president and this administration has really struggled to make a sustained, compelling case for this war.
sentences.cn: 总统和本届政府确实很难为这场战争提出持续且令人信服的理由。
sentences.structure: 主语 (The president and this administration) + 谓语 (has struggled) + 不定式短语 (to make a sustained, compelling case for this war)
sentences.analysis: 注意主语是复数，但谓语用了 'has'，可能是笔误或口语化表达。'make a case for' 意为“提出理由支持”。

sentences.en: Because the U.S. has obliterated Iran's military.
sentences.cn: 因为美国已经摧毁了伊朗的军队。
sentences.structure: 连词 (Because) + 主语 (the U.S.) + 谓语 (has obliterated) + 宾语 (Iran's military)
sentences.analysis: 这是一个原因状语从句，单独成句，在上下文中是对前面问题的解释。

sentences.en: And this goes back to the same contradiction around the nuclear program.
sentences.cn: 这又回到了围绕核计划的同一个矛盾。
sentences.structure: 主语 (This) + 谓语 (goes back to) + 宾语 (the same contradiction) + 介词短语 (around the nuclear program)
sentences.analysis: 'go back to' 意为“追溯到，回到”。'around' 表示“关于，围绕”。

sentences.en: Democrats are starting to really lean into the terminology 'war of choice.'
sentences.cn: 民主党人开始真正倾向于使用“选择性战争”这一术语。
sentences.structure: 主语 (Democrats) + 谓语 (are starting) + 不定式短语 (to really lean into the terminology 'war of choice.')
sentences.analysis: 'lean into' 是口语化短语，意为“倾向于，投入”。'terminology' 后跟同位语 'war of choice'。

sentences.en: They're facing an extradition request from the U.K. on charges including rape and sex trafficking.
sentences.cn: 他们面临英国提出的引渡请求，指控包括强奸和性贩卖。
sentences.structure: 主语 (They) + 谓语 (are facing) + 宾语 (an extradition request) + 介词短语 (from the U.K.) + 介词短语 (on charges including rape and sex trafficking)
sentences.analysis: 'on charges' 意为“以...指控”。'including' 是现在分词，引导短语修饰 'charges'。

sentences.en: I don't know if they're going to turn a blind eye or not.
sentences.cn: 我不知道他们是否会视而不见。
sentences.structure: 主语 (I) + 谓语 (don't know) + 宾语从句 (if they're going to turn a blind eye or not)
sentences.analysis: 'if' 引导宾语从句，意为“是否”。'turn a blind eye' 是固定短语，意为“视而不见”。

sentences.en: But I think that there's no way, to Julia's point, that this is something that is -- we should be thinking about as more than a gimmick.
sentences.cn: 但我认为，正如朱莉娅所说，这绝不仅仅是一个噱头。
sentences.structure: 主语 (I) + 谓语 (think) + 宾语从句 (that there's no way, to Julia's point, that this is something that is -- we should be thinking about as more than a gimmick)
sentences.analysis: 'to Julia's point' 是插入语，意为“正如朱莉娅所说”。'that this is something...' 是同位语从句，解释 'way'。破折号表示停顿或修正。

sentences.en: Can you run a long-term mayoral tenure while polarizing literally half the city?
sentences.cn: 你能在让半个城市两极分化的情况下长期担任市长吗？
sentences.structure: 情态动词 (Can) + 主语 (you) + 谓语 (run) + 宾语 (a long-term mayoral tenure) + 时间状语从句 (while polarizing literally half the city)
sentences.analysis: 'while polarizing...' 是现在分词短语作时间状语，相当于 'while you polarize...'。'literally' 用于强调。

sentences.en: So, he's continuing, even if it's not legal, to tap into that.
sentences.cn: 所以，即使不合法，他也在继续利用这一点。
sentences.structure: 连词 (So) + 主语 (he) + 谓语 (is continuing) + 插入语 (even if it's not legal) + 不定式短语 (to tap into that)
sentences.analysis: 'even if it's not legal' 是让步状语从句，插入在谓语和不定式之间。'tap into' 意为“利用”。

topics.title: US-Iran Conflict and Military Escalation
topics.content: The transcript discusses the ongoing US military strikes against Iran and Iran's retaliatory attacks, including the death of US service members. The conflict has expanded to include targets in Kuwait, Bahrain, and other Gulf states. The US aims to prevent Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons, while Iran responds with tit-for-tat strikes. The Strait of Hormuz remains a key point of contention.
topics.keywords: Iran, US military, retaliation, Strait of Hormuz, nuclear program, tit-for-tat

topics.title: Domestic Political Reactions to the War
topics.content: The transcript covers how the Trump administration is struggling to make a compelling case for the war to the American public. Democrats are framing it as a 'war of choice,' and even some Republican figures like Maria Bartiromo and Marjorie Taylor Greene are questioning the logic of the conflict. The human and economic costs, including rising gas prices, are becoming central issues.
topics.keywords: war of choice, public opinion, gas prices, midterms, Republican, Democrat

topics.title: Tate Brothers Arrest and Extradition
topics.content: Andrew and Tristan Tate were arrested in Miami on a UK extradition warrant for charges including rape and sex trafficking. The brothers have a large online following and previously faced similar charges in Romania. The Trump administration's past involvement in their return to the US is noted, raising questions about potential political interference.
topics.keywords: Tate brothers, extradition, sex trafficking, rape, UK, Romania, manosphere

topics.title: New York Mayor's Threat to Arrest Netanyahu
topics.content: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani stated he is exploring the legal possibility of arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu if he visits New York for the UN General Assembly, citing an ICC warrant. The move is seen as a polarizing political stance, appealing to progressive voters but risking backlash from Jewish constituents and business leaders.
topics.keywords: Netanyahu, ICC, arrest warrant, New York, Mamdani, Israel, UN

topics.title: Legionnaires' Disease Outbreak in New York
topics.content: The transcript briefly mentions a growing outbreak of Legionnaires' disease in New York, a serious lung infection caused by Legionella bacteria.
topics.keywords: Legionnaires' disease, outbreak, New York, public health

raw_transcript:
Return to Transcripts main page

3 U.S. Troops Killed in Deadliest Week of Iran Since March; Tate Brothers Arrested in Miami on U.K. Rape, Trafficking Charges. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 20, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: Reporting that the canine was in the water. They used a drone to locate the tiny pooch. The dog is now safe and back with its owners.

Well, that does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS here. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Today in the group chat, Trump supporters tolerated high gas prices. Will they stand by the war in Iran if more U.S. troops are killed in action?

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: Well, we feel very badly. But you know, those great people, those great patriots, were out there fighting that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon.

CORNISH: And Mayor Zohran Mamdani wants to have Israel's Benjamin Netanyahu arrested if he visits New York. How would that work?

And the Tate brothers under arrest in Miami? Will the Trump administration hand them over to the U.K. to face charges of rape and sex trafficking?

And that Qatari jet that's been serving as Air Force One will be sent back for more security upgrades. Were the White House leakers right?

TRUMP: Iran has been very, very badly damaged. They've lost everything almost militarily.

CORNISH: The U.S. lost at least three service members this weekend, the deadliest week of the war for the Pentagon since March.

Good morning everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and that is where we begin. Fears of a wider war in the Middle East.

The U.S. military has ramped up attacks targeting Iranian roads, tunnels, railroads, and, according to Iranian media, a plant for drinking water.

Now, in return, Iran expanded its list of targets across seven countries, including a U.S. base in Jordan, where two service members were killed Friday. Another is missing.

One U.S. service member also died in Northern Iraq Sunday while trying to detonate a downed Iranian attack drone.

MARCO RUBIO, U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE: We shot down almost all the missiles. One leaked through, hit the wrong place, and tragically. And it's heartbreaking.

And our hearts go out to these families. These are heroes that paid the ultimate price. Nothing the military does is safe. It's all inherently dangerous. And we're just grateful to have such heroic Americans in uniform, in the service of our country.

CORNISH: Joining me now to discuss is CNN's Leila Gharagozlou. She is in Abu Dhabi.

And Leila, at this point, it's pretty clear that the diplomatic path has been cut off. Can you talk about what attacks have been seen in the region, the allies that are bearing the brunt of it?

LEILA GHARAGOZLOU, CNN CORRESPONDENT: Yes. So, Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of the Iranian retaliations to these U.S. strikes.

Now in Iran, the U.S. is striking the Southern region, which is considered to be Iran's defensive arch. The coastal cities, the islands. These are really key to Iran's ability to control the Strait of Hormuz.

And that is where the U.S. has really focused its strikes over the last nine days.

That being said, today the Iranian state media did say that there were explosions heard near Tabriz in Northwestern Iran, and that's the first time in these nine days that we've really seen the U.S. strikes go to that area of Iran and kind of move more inward.

Whereas earlier in this war, a lot of the U.S. and Israeli strikes were really focused on the major cities like Tehran and Isfahan.

So, we're seeing kind of an expansion within Iran. And the Iranians, for their part, have said -- the IRGC said over the weekend through various outlets -- that they will respond in kind. So, if the U.S. escalates and expands, the Iranians will also escalate

and expand. And that is so far what we're seeing. It's very much a tit-for-tat. So, if the U.S. hits, you know, any sort of civilian infrastructure, the Iranians are going to do the same.

And as I said, Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of this in the GCC, more so than they had been in the first part of this war, where we did see the Iranian retaliation kind of extend out to places like the UAE, as well -- Audie.

CORNISH: We heard Mojtaba Khamenei, the -- Iran's supreme leader, say over the weekend, "The memorandum signed between the presidents of Iran and the U.S. once again proved to everyone how worthless and unreliable the signature of the U.S. president is."

What does this mean for the Strait of Hormuz, which was a key part of that memorandum agreement?

GHARAGOZLOU: Well, so the Iranians say that the strait is in their control and still closed. President Trump, as we heard, has said that they are in control of the strait. So, it's still very disputed. And the U.S. is, as I said, very actively trying to loosen Iran's control over that strait.

Now, the MOU had initially, essentially, opened up the strait based on one of the articles in there. But with the MOU now in tatters, there's no way to kind of enforce that piece of the agreement.

So, we're going to have to see whether Iran wants to come back to the negotiating table, if the U.S. comes back to the negotiating table and through that, open the Strait of Hormuz.

It does seem very unlikely that the U.S. will be able to get the strait opened through force. It hasn't worked yet, but that's obviously what they're trying to do.

CORNISH: OK. Leila, thanks so much for talking with us.

I want to turn now to the group chat: Sara Fischer, CNN media -- senior media analyst and senior media reporter at Axios; Seung Min Kim, CNN political analyst and White House reporter for the Associated Press; and Julia Manchester, national political reporter at "The Hill."

Seung Min, can I talk to you about how the White House has tried to talk about troop deaths? I think we're coming up on upwards of 17 members --

SEUNG MIN KIM, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST: Right, 17.

CORNISH: -- service members who have died during this conflict, and the president pretty much is saying, in a way, to me, It's a war. This is going to happen. They're fighting for a good cause. What else are you hearing?

KIM: Right. I mean, that has been the thrust of the president's message and so, therefore, the broader White House's message that, while these troop deaths are tragic -- and you saw that in the clip that we aired earlier -- that this is all -- they're doing it because they're doing it for a cause that they -- that -- that is a worthy one, which is that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon.

Now, it's unclear whether that case that he's making to the American people is actually being effective, because he has said the same thing that, as, you know, related to gas prices.

He's saying these higher gas prices are a cost that the American people are willing to bear, because it is in service of a broader goal that is worthy.

Polling shows that's not necessarily the case.

KIM: And the president and this administration has really struggled to make a sustained, compelling case for this war. And you need to make a sustained, compelling case for the war, because, A, the public is becoming more and more weary of conflicts in general.

CORNISH: I was going to say, you need to make it, because there's a lot of people poking holes.

CORNISH: Because there hasn't been a sustained one.

CORNISH: I just want to point out one, Maria Baritomo [SIC] -- Bartiromo -- Sorry, Maria -- who's been asking questions about how is Iran able to strike back if it's decimated. And like, that's before you even get to the greater just cause. People are asking questions of logic. Here's what she had to say over the weekend.

MARIA BARTIROMO, FOX NEWS HOST: But I don't understand how it's possible that Iran has the capabilities to fight this way and continue to try to lash out at Gulf countries and U.S. military bases. Because the U.S. has obliterated Iran's military. And the president continues to reiterate that.

CORNISH: I mean, that's pretty basic.

SARA FISCHER, CNN SENIOR MEDIA ANALYST: Yes.

CORNISH: If it's obliterated, why are we still in a fight? Yes. And this goes back to the same contradiction around the nuclear

program. Remember when the Trump administration had come out and said that the nuclear program was dead?

And now all this war, the purpose of it, is making sure that Iran cannot enrich its uranium to develop a nuclear program.

CORNISH: And it is still sitting on enriched --

CORNISH: -- uranium, which the president is insisting on calling "dust," again to try and evoke the idea of something being obliterated. But you don't fight like this if there's nothing but dust left.

JULIA MANCHESTER, NATIONAL POLITICAL REPORTER, "THE HILL": No, you don't. And look, when you have someone like Maria Bartiromo saying that to a FOX News audience, that speaks -- speaks volumes.

And it really comes at a time, really, particularly in the Senate and the House, when you have so many priorities trying to be pushed through, including the NDAA, for example, the National Defense Authorization Act, that's not --

CORNISH: Meaning the budget.

MANCHESTER: The budget, yes.

CORNISH: And they're asking for, what, $1 trillion?

MANCHESTER: Yes. For defense spending. And that's an issue, I think, for Republicans right now, because you have Democrats starting to oppose it.

But now, if you have a broader Republican audience looking at this and saying, look, what's the cost of this war, the human cost, the economic cost? I mean, that creates issues for that party.

FISCHER: One thing I'm watching, Democrats are starting to really lean into the terminology "war of choice." This is something that --

FISCHER: -- Senator Mark Warner is coming out and pushing. They want to make sure, heading into the midterms, that the public understands that this is not a necessary war, from their point of view, and that this war of choice is not only, you know, impacting people's lives, but it's impacting them at the gas pumps. It's impacting the economy broadly.

CORNISH: Yes. To give an example, Marjorie Taylor Greene, former congresswoman, doing a social media post saying, "Sacrifice? What are you talking about? They did not willingly sacrifice themselves. They were killed because of the war you, Trump, and the administration are waging on behalf of a foreign country, Israel." FISCHER: And just last thing, the death of the late Senator Lindsey Graham also plays in here, because Graham was one of the few war hawks who sort of, you know, supported the president, had his back when it came to this.

And now you have him removed. You have Republicans; Maria Bartiromo in the media; Marjorie Taylor Greene, formerly --

FISCHER: -- a member of Congress, who are coming out and putting pressure on the administration. It is not --

CORNISH: But losing that high-profile voice that's going to defend him in all arenas --

CORNISH: -- has been a problem.

CORNISH: OK, you guys, stay with us. We're going to talk more, this time to a retired Air Force colonel. Is the Pentagon prepared to continue this fight? Does the U.S. have the troops, the hardware necessary for a long-term conflict?

And in the meantime, gas prices are rising again as this war rages on. Experts say they're not coming down anytime soon.

[06:15:04] CORNISH: It's almost 15 minutes past the hour. This is your morning roundup.

Spain is now World Cup champions. They beat defending champs Argentina with a single goal in overtime. The final match was set in New Jersey. President Trump watched in the stands and delivered the trophy to the winners.

TRUMP: We're really happy that it did so well. And all over the world, people have come, and they've loved our country, you know, shown off our country in a different light.

CORNISH: Thirty-nine-year-old Messi fell short of pulling off what few teams have done before: a back-to-back championship. He says this is his last World Cup.

And the administration welcomes -- welcomes its newest member, the vice president announcing his wife, Usha Vance, welcomed their fourth child. Baby Alec Neel Vance is the first newborn to a sitting vice president's family in more than 150 years. He is the couple's third boy.

And today, the national average for gas prices is back at $4 per gallon. It's just the second time we've hit that benchmark since the war with Iran.

The first time was back in March, a month into the strikes. And experts say prices are not coming down any time soon.

And the Tate brothers expected in federal court this week after their arrest in Miami on Saturday. They're facing an extradition request from the U.K. on charges including rape and sex trafficking.

The social media influencers have built a massive following with content claiming about dominating women, and they face similar charges in Romania. The brothers deny the claims.

I have to bring this to a group chat full of women. Not -- not because we're chuckling, but because they were allowed back in the U.S., right?

And at the time, that was a controversy. But the U.K. seems to be feeling differently. And what does this have to do with the big "New Yorker" profile that basically exposed a lot of the way they operated abroad?

FISCHER: When -- in 2025, when they left Romania, there were reports that Trump administration officials helped with that process, in particular Ric Grenell.

So, you take a look at what's happening now, and you don't think that this administration is going to necessarily -- I don't see them stepping in right now, but they've stepped in in the past.

I think what it means is that, on a global stage, you have these countries, the U.K. and Romania, who are taking this pretty seriously. And here in the united states, this does not seem to be an issue that at least our political figures are putting forth.

CORNISH: I think it's just weird, because the -- especially the Republican Party has talked about the ways they've handled sex traffickers and being tough.

FISCHER: A hundred percent.

CORNISH: And it is -- it feels like, are we going to be in a position where the U.S. is defending somebody, interfering in investigation of some kind of someone accused of sex trafficking because of their connections to like, the political manosphere? FISCHER: Yes. I mean, the Trump administration obviously values the

audience that the Tate brothers have. It spans millions and millions of followers across platforms.

But the irony here, of course, is that it was the Republicans who were all in on Epstein child sex trafficking. This is the issue that really matters. And then now you see some of the administration. I don't know if they're going to turn a blind eye or not.

FISCHER: You know, I look at what happened in 2025. It leads me to believe that they probably will, when it comes to targeting somebody whose audience is valuable to them.

CORNISH: Yes. But there's also new leadership in the U.K. as of today. So, very interesting to see how this is going to play out.

I want to turn to something else after the break. New York's Mayor Mamdani, he wants Benjamin Netanyahu arrested if Israel's leader comes to New York. So, we want to know how that might work.

Plus, Democrats in Maine moving fast. We'll show you who has emerged as the candidate for Senate after Graham Platner's campaign collapsed.

And good morning to our viewers in Detroit. This is a live look at the sunrise over downtown.

CORNISH: So, could the mayor of New York City have Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu arrested? He says he's looking into it. The Israeli leader is expected to attend the U.N. General Assembly in New York in September.

Speaking on "The New York Times," "The Interview" podcast, Mayor Mamdani said he's having, quote, "active conversations" about what the law allows him to do.

LULU GARCIA, NAVARRO, HOST, "THE INTERVIEW": You're actively looking to see if you can actually arrest him?

MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D), NEW YORK CITY): I think we are looking at all of our legal obligations, and one of them is with regards to the United Nations.

But the other part of this is, is we're not talking about a personal assessment of Benjamin Netanyahu. We're talking about the International Criminal Court and the fact that they have a warrant out for this prime minister's arrest.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: OK. And that's one of our group chatters there, Lulu Garcia- Navarro, who is the host of "The Interview."

First, what do we know about what is possible, Julia?

MANCHESTER: Right, right. So, this does not appear on its face to be possible, because according to International Criminal Court proceedings, you have to -- a country has had to agree to the Rome Statute to basically, you know, arrest someone with an arrest.

CORNISH: Right. And the U.S., notoriously, is not all that interested in participating in the ICC.

MANCHESTER: Yes, absolutely. We know that the Trump administration and the Biden administration have notoriously not great relationships with the ICC.

That being said, though, I do find these comments fascinating, because we are seeing really a decline in support of Israel from Americans across the board, across the political board.

On the far left, or the left, you have someone like Zohran Mamdani talking about arresting Benjamin Netanyahu. But this comes roughly a week after we saw Vice President J.D. Vance go on Joe Rogan's podcast and essentially accuse Israel of manipulating U.S. public opinion over Iran.

CORNISH: We were talking about that.

And I think the thing for me that's confusing, though, is these other people don't have this, like, very large Jewish constituency that has been --

CORNISH: -- active, activist, and upset with the way he has talked about this issue.

And I believe it was a campaign promise. But I looked at a website that had dozens of campaign promises, and most of them weren't fulfilled. Why would this be one that he would kind of talk about publicly?

FISCHER: It's a strange political battle to pick, especially because you think about a lot of the Jewish business leaders in New York who business leaders have threatened to leave the city.

CORNISH: But is that, like, a gotcha moment? Like, is there no way he could have said, actually, that's not something I would do, because he had promised it. Did he basically have to answer that way, because it was "The New York Times" raising it?

FISCHER: Oh, so you're saying, like, was he put into a corner, and he has to give his most polarizing take -- CORNISH: Yes.

FISCHER: -- because of -- I mean, that's kind of been his whole campaign shtick, right? Like, putting out these incredibly sort of polarizing positions.

A lot of people have questioned whether or not they're even really possible. Right? And then trying to utilize that to gain public support over one group within New York.

The question becomes, Audie, to your point, New York has very different constituencies. And can you run a long-term mayoral tenure while polarizing literally half the city? Like, answer is to be seen.

But I think that there's no way, to Julia's point, that this is something that is -- we should be thinking about as more than a gimmick.

CORNISH: Right. At the end of the day, he can't run for president, right? So, he's not thinking of, like, those big, long-term ambitions.

But he is, to me, coming off as somebody who is part of a movement.

CORNISH: And trying to be at the forefront of a movement --

CORNISH: -- that requires coming up with sentences to say, right, as a Democrat or somebody who is progressive, that will appeal to this world of the newly polled that are frustrated.

KIM: Right, right. You're right that he can't run for president, but he does have a -- he does have a constituency that is more than just New York.

He does have a national political following that is very interested in what he does and very interested in his influence. And he's very interested in flexing that influence.

KIM: Obviously, we saw a lot of that in the New York races. Who knows whether that expands in the future?

But this is part of -- this is an issue that is so critical to the base of the Democratic Party. So, he's continuing, even if it's not legal, to tap into that.

CORNISH: OK. Coming up, I want to talk about President Trump's new Air Force One. It's already headed back to the shop. Turns out it does need some more security work.

Plus, a deadly Legionnaires outbreak is growing in New York.

segments.title: CNN.com - Transcripts
segments.text:
Return to Transcripts main page

3 U.S. Troops Killed in Deadliest Week of Iran Since March; Tate Brothers Arrested in Miami on U.K. Rape, Trafficking Charges. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 20, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

BRAD SMITH, CNN ANCHOR: Reporting that the canine was in the water. They used a drone to locate the tiny pooch. The dog is now safe and back with its owners.

Well, that does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS here. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Today in the group chat, Trump supporters tolerated high gas prices. Will they stand by the war in Iran if more U.S. troops are killed in action?

DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT: Well, we feel very badly. But you know, those great people, those great patriots, were out there fighting that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon.

CORNISH: And Mayor Zohran Mamdani wants to have Israel's Benjamin Netanyahu arrested if he visits New York. How would that work?

And the Tate brothers under arrest in Miami? Will the Trump administration hand them over to the U.K. to face charges of rape and sex trafficking?

And that Qatari jet that's been serving as Air Force One will be sent back for more security upgrades. Were the White House leakers right?

TRUMP: Iran has been very, very badly damaged. They've lost everything almost militarily.

CORNISH: The U.S. lost at least three service members this weekend, the deadliest week of the war for the Pentagon since March.

Good morning everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and that is where we begin. Fears of a wider war in the Middle East.

The U.S. military has ramped up attacks targeting Iranian roads, tunnels, railroads, and, according to Iranian media, a plant for drinking water.

Now, in return, Iran expanded its list of targets across seven countries, including a U.S. base in Jordan, where two service members were killed Friday. Another is missing.

One U.S. service member also died in Northern Iraq Sunday while trying to detonate a downed Iranian attack drone.

MARCO RUBIO, U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE: We shot down almost all the missiles. One leaked through, hit the wrong place, and tragically. And it's heartbreaking.

And our hearts go out to these families. These are heroes that paid the ultimate price. Nothing the military does is safe. It's all inherently dangerous. And we're just grateful to have such heroic Americans in uniform, in the service of our country.

CORNISH: Joining me now to discuss is CNN's Leila Gharagozlou. She is in Abu Dhabi.

And Leila, at this point, it's pretty clear that the diplomatic path has been cut off. Can you talk about what attacks have been seen in the region, the allies that are bearing the brunt of it?

LEILA GHARAGOZLOU, CNN CORRESPONDENT: Yes. So, Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of the Iranian retaliations to these U.S. strikes.

Now in Iran, the U.S. is striking the Southern region, which is considered to be Iran's defensive arch. The coastal cities, the islands. These are really key to Iran's ability to control the Strait of Hormuz.

And that is where the U.S. has really focused its strikes over the last nine days.

That being said, today the Iranian state media did say that there were explosions heard near Tabriz in Northwestern Iran, and that's the first time in these nine days that we've really seen the U.S. strikes go to that area of Iran and kind of move more inward.

Whereas earlier in this war, a lot of the U.S. and Israeli strikes were really focused on the major cities like Tehran and Isfahan.

So, we're seeing kind of an expansion within Iran. And the Iranians, for their part, have said -- the IRGC said over the weekend through various outlets -- that they will respond in kind. So, if the U.S. escalates and expands, the Iranians will also escalate

and expand. And that is so far what we're seeing. It's very much a tit-for-tat. So, if the U.S. hits, you know, any sort of civilian infrastructure, the Iranians are going to do the same.

And as I said, Kuwait and Bahrain are really bearing the brunt of this in the GCC, more so than they had been in the first part of this war, where we did see the Iranian retaliation kind of extend out to places like the UAE, as well -- Audie.

CORNISH: We heard Mojtaba Khamenei, the -- Iran's supreme leader, say over the weekend, "The memorandum signed between the presidents of Iran and the U.S. once again proved to everyone how worthless and unreliable the signature of the U.S. president is."

What does this mean for the Strait of Hormuz, which was a key part of that memorandum agreement?

GHARAGOZLOU: Well, so the Iranians say that the strait is in their control and still closed. President Trump, as we heard, has said that they are in control of the strait. So, it's still very disputed. And the U.S. is, as I said, very actively trying to loosen Iran's control over that strait.

Now, the MOU had initially, essentially, opened up the strait based on one of the articles in there. But with the MOU now in tatters, there's no way to kind of enforce that piece of the agreement.

So, we're going to have to see whether Iran wants to come back to the negotiating table, if the U.S. comes back to the negotiating table and through that, open the Strait of Hormuz.

It does seem very unlikely that the U.S. will be able to get the strait opened through force. It hasn't worked yet, but that's obviously what they're trying to do.

CORNISH: OK. Leila, thanks so much for talking with us.

I want to turn now to the group chat: Sara Fischer, CNN media -- senior media analyst and senior media reporter at Axios; Seung Min Kim, CNN political analyst and White House reporter for the Associated Press; and Julia Manchester, national political reporter at "The Hill."

Seung Min, can I talk to you about how the White House has tried to talk about troop deaths? I think we're coming up on upwards of 17 members --

SEUNG MIN KIM, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST: Right, 17.

CORNISH: -- service members who have died during this conflict, and the president pretty much is saying, in a way, to me, It's a war. This is going to happen. They're fighting for a good cause. What else are you hearing?

KIM: Right. I mean, that has been the thrust of the president's message and so, therefore, the broader White House's message that, while these troop deaths are tragic -- and you saw that in the clip that we aired earlier -- that this is all -- they're doing it because they're doing it for a cause that they -- that -- that is a worthy one, which is that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon.

Now, it's unclear whether that case that he's making to the American people is actually being effective, because he has said the same thing that, as, you know, related to gas prices.

He's saying these higher gas prices are a cost that the American people are willing to bear, because it is in service of a broader goal that is worthy.

Polling shows that's not necessarily the case.

KIM: And the president and this administration has really struggled to make a sustained, compelling case for this war. And you need to make a sustained, compelling case for the war, because, A, the public is becoming more and more weary of conflicts in general.

CORNISH: I was going to say, you need to make it, because there's a lot of people poking holes.

CORNISH: Because there hasn't been a sustained one.

CORNISH: I just want to point out one, Maria Baritomo [SIC] -- Bartiromo -- Sorry, Maria -- who's been asking questions about how is Iran able to strike back if it's decimated. And like, that's before you even get to the greater just cause. People are asking questions of logic. Here's what she had to say over the weekend.

MARIA BARTIROMO, FOX NEWS HOST: But I don't understand how it's possible that Iran has the capabilities to fight this way and continue to try to lash out at Gulf countries and U.S. military bases. Because the U.S. has obliterated Iran's military. And the president continues to reiterate that.

CORNISH: I mean, that's pretty basic.

SARA FISCHER, CNN SENIOR MEDIA ANALYST: Yes.

CORNISH: If it's obliterated, why are we still in a fight? Yes. And this goes back to the same contradiction around the nuclear

program. Remember when the Trump administration had come out and said that the nuclear program was dead?

And now all this war, the purpose of it, is making sure that Iran cannot enrich its uranium to develop a nuclear program.

CORNISH: And it is still sitting on enriched --

CORNISH: -- uranium, which the president is insisting on calling "dust," again to try and evoke the idea of something being obliterated. But you don't fight like this if there's nothing but dust left.

JULIA MANCHESTER, NATIONAL POLITICAL REPORTER, "THE HILL": No, you don't. And look, when you have someone like Maria Bartiromo saying that to a FOX News audience, that speaks -- speaks volumes.

And it really comes at a time, really, particularly in the Senate and the House, when you have so many priorities trying to be pushed through, including the NDAA, for example, the National Defense Authorization Act, that's not --

CORNISH: Meaning the budget.

MANCHESTER: The budget, yes.

CORNISH: And they're asking for, what, $1 trillion?

MANCHESTER: Yes. For defense spending. And that's an issue, I think, for Republicans right now, because you have Democrats starting to oppose it.

But now, if you have a broader Republican audience looking at this and saying, look, what's the cost of this war, the human cost, the economic cost? I mean, that creates issues for that party.

FISCHER: One thing I'm watching, Democrats are starting to really lean into the terminology "war of choice." This is something that --

FISCHER: -- Senator Mark Warner is coming out and pushing. They want to make sure, heading into the midterms, that the public understands that this is not a necessary war, from their point of view, and that this war of choice is not only, you know, impacting people's lives, but it's impacting them at the gas pumps. It's impacting the economy broadly.

CORNISH: Yes. To give an example, Marjorie Taylor Greene, former congresswoman, doing a social media post saying, "Sacrifice? What are you talking about? They did not willingly sacrifice themselves. They were killed because of the war you, Trump, and the administration are waging on behalf of a foreign country, Israel." FISCHER: And just last thing, the death of the late Senator Lindsey Graham also plays in here, because Graham was one of the few war hawks who sort of, you know, supported the president, had his back when it came to this.

And now you have him removed. You have Republicans; Maria Bartiromo in the media; Marjorie Taylor Greene, formerly --

FISCHER: -- a member of Congress, who are coming out and putting pressure on the administration. It is not --

CORNISH: But losing that high-profile voice that's going to defend him in all arenas --

CORNISH: -- has been a problem.

CORNISH: OK, you guys, stay with us. We're going to talk more, this time to a retired Air Force colonel. Is the Pentagon prepared to continue this fight? Does the U.S. have the troops, the hardware necessary for a long-term conflict?

And in the meantime, gas prices are rising again as this war rages on. Experts say they're not coming down anytime soon.

[06:15:04] CORNISH: It's almost 15 minutes past the hour. This is your morning roundup.

Spain is now World Cup champions. They beat defending champs Argentina with a single goal in overtime. The final match was set in New Jersey. President Trump watched in the stands and delivered the trophy to the winners.

TRUMP: We're really happy that it did so well. And all over the world, people have come, and they've loved our country, you know, shown off our country in a different light.

CORNISH: Thirty-nine-year-old Messi fell short of pulling off what few teams have done before: a back-to-back championship. He says this is his last World Cup.

And the administration welcomes -- welcomes its newest member, the vice president announcing his wife, Usha Vance, welcomed their fourth child. Baby Alec Neel Vance is the first newborn to a sitting vice president's family in more than 150 years. He is the couple's third boy.

And today, the national average for gas prices is back at $4 per gallon. It's just the second time we've hit that benchmark since the war with Iran.

The first time was back in March, a month into the strikes. And experts say prices are not coming down any time soon.

And the Tate brothers expected in federal court this week after their arrest in Miami on Saturday. They're facing an extradition request from the U.K. on charges including rape and sex trafficking.

The social media influencers have built a massive following with content claiming about dominating women, and they face similar charges in Romania. The brothers deny the claims.

I have to bring this to a group chat full of women. Not -- not because we're chuckling, but because they were allowed back in the U.S., right?

And at the time, that was a controversy. But the U.K. seems to be feeling differently. And what does this have to do with the big "New Yorker" profile that basically exposed a lot of the way they operated abroad?

FISCHER: When -- in 2025, when they left Romania, there were reports that Trump administration officials helped with that process, in particular Ric Grenell.

So, you take a look at what's happening now, and you don't think that this administration is going to necessarily -- I don't see them stepping in right now, but they've stepped in in the past.

I think what it means is that, on a global stage, you have these countries, the U.K. and Romania, who are taking this pretty seriously. And here in the united states, this does not seem to be an issue that at least our political figures are putting forth.

CORNISH: I think it's just weird, because the -- especially the Republican Party has talked about the ways they've handled sex traffickers and being tough.

FISCHER: A hundred percent.

CORNISH: And it is -- it feels like, are we going to be in a position where the U.S. is defending somebody, interfering in investigation of some kind of someone accused of sex trafficking because of their connections to like, the political manosphere? FISCHER: Yes. I mean, the Trump administration obviously values the

audience that the Tate brothers have. It spans millions and millions of followers across platforms.

But the irony here, of course, is that it was the Republicans who were all in on Epstein child sex trafficking. This is the issue that really matters. And then now you see some of the administration. I don't know if they're going to turn a blind eye or not.

FISCHER: You know, I look at what happened in 2025. It leads me to believe that they probably will, when it comes to targeting somebody whose audience is valuable to them.

CORNISH: Yes. But there's also new leadership in the U.K. as of today. So, very interesting to see how this is going to play out.

I want to turn to something else after the break. New York's Mayor Mamdani, he wants Benjamin Netanyahu arrested if Israel's leader comes to New York. So, we want to know how that might work.

Plus, Democrats in Maine moving fast. We'll show you who has emerged as the candidate for Senate after Graham Platner's campaign collapsed.

And good morning to our viewers in Detroit. This is a live look at the sunrise over downtown.

CORNISH: So, could the mayor of New York City have Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu arrested? He says he's looking into it. The Israeli leader is expected to attend the U.N. General Assembly in New York in September.

Speaking on "The New York Times," "The Interview" podcast, Mayor Mamdani said he's having, quote, "active conversations" about what the law allows him to do.

LULU GARCIA, NAVARRO, HOST, "THE INTERVIEW": You're actively looking to see if you can actually arrest him?

MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D), NEW YORK CITY): I think we are looking at all of our legal obligations, and one of them is with regards to the United Nations.

But the other part of this is, is we're not talking about a personal assessment of Benjamin Netanyahu. We're talking about the International Criminal Court and the fact that they have a warrant out for this prime minister's arrest.

(END VIDEO CLIP) CORNISH: OK. And that's one of our group chatters there, Lulu Garcia- Navarro, who is the host of "The Interview."

First, what do we know about what is possible, Julia?

MANCHESTER: Right, right. So, this does not appear on its face to be possible, because according to International Criminal Court proceedings, you have to -- a country has had to agree to the Rome Statute to basically, you know, arrest someone with an arrest.

CORNISH: Right. And the U.S., notoriously, is not all that interested in participating in the ICC.

MANCHESTER: Yes, absolutely. We know that the Trump administration and the Biden administration have notoriously not great relationships with the ICC.

That being said, though, I do find these comments fascinating, because we are seeing really a decline in support of Israel from Americans across the board, across the political board.

On the far left, or the left, you have someone like Zohran Mamdani talking about arresting Benjamin Netanyahu. But this comes roughly a week after we saw Vice President J.D. Vance go on Joe Rogan's podcast and essentially accuse Israel of manipulating U.S. public opinion over Iran.

CORNISH: We were talking about that.

And I think the thing for me that's confusing, though, is these other people don't have this, like, very large Jewish constituency that has been --

CORNISH: -- active, activist, and upset with the way he has talked about this issue.

And I believe it was a campaign promise. But I looked at a website that had dozens of campaign promises, and most of them weren't fulfilled. Why would this be one that he would kind of talk about publicly?

FISCHER: It's a strange political battle to pick, especially because you think about a lot of the Jewish business leaders in New York who business leaders have threatened to leave the city.

CORNISH: But is that, like, a gotcha moment? Like, is there no way he could have said, actually, that's not something I would do, because he had promised it. Did he basically have to answer that way, because it was "The New York Times" raising it?

FISCHER: Oh, so you're saying, like, was he put into a corner, and he has to give his most polarizing take -- CORNISH: Yes.

FISCHER: -- because of -- I mean, that's kind of been his whole campaign shtick, right? Like, putting out these incredibly sort of polarizing positions.

A lot of people have questioned whether or not they're even really possible. Right? And then trying to utilize that to gain public support over one group within New York.

The question becomes, Audie, to your point, New York has very different constituencies. And can you run a long-term mayoral tenure while polarizing literally half the city? Like, answer is to be seen.

But I think that there's no way, to Julia's point, that this is something that is -- we should be thinking about as more than a gimmick.

CORNISH: Right. At the end of the day, he can't run for president, right? So, he's not thinking of, like, those big, long-term ambitions.

But he is, to me, coming off as somebody who is part of a movement.

CORNISH: And trying to be at the forefront of a movement --

CORNISH: -- that requires coming up with sentences to say, right, as a Democrat or somebody who is progressive, that will appeal to this world of the newly polled that are frustrated.

KIM: Right, right. You're right that he can't run for president, but he does have a -- he does have a constituency that is more than just New York.
